title: Inside AI-pilled engineering teams: Five lessons for scaling without losing the plot
gist: BVP research finds 90% of tech/engineering teams now deploy AI core to operations, and distills five operating lessons from Ramp, Shopify, and operating advisors: decouple velocity from release risk with two tiers, standardize the infra layer (LLM proxy) not the tools, treat agentic harnesses as the 2026 move, hire stage-specific engineering leadership, and manage the new 'comprehension debt' from AI-speed shipping.

insights.claim: Bessemer's research finds 90% of tech and engineering teams are now deploying AI as core to their operations, with top use cases being code generation (92%), code review augmentation (79%), AI-powered product features (75%), documentation generation (69%), and agentic development (60%).
insights.speaker: Christine Deakers
insights.so_what: Quantifies that AI in engineering has crossed from experiment to default; dev-tool vendors selling code-gen and review augmentation are at the largest TAM, agentic still earliest (60%) and growing.
insights.quote: Bessemer found that 90% of tech and engineering teams are deploying AI or its core to their operations.
insights.locator: In our latest research on how companies are using AI today

insights.claim: Among engineering leaders, the top AI adoption pain points are evaluating code quality (52%), measuring productivity gains (46%), managing token costs (38%), and security/IP concerns (29%).
insights.speaker: Christine Deakers
insights.so_what: Maps the white space for new dev-infra startups: code-quality eval, productivity measurement, token-cost governance, and AI security/IP are the unsolved buyer problems VCs should fund into.
insights.quote: 52% of leaders cite evaluating code quality as a top challenge, followed by measuring productivity gains (46%), managing token costs (38%), and addressing security and IP concerns (29%).
insights.locator: But adoption at scale creates new challenges

insights.claim: Ramp ships major new features daily by decoupling shipping velocity from release risk: any team can ship to an early-access tier (≈10% of customers, a built-in 5,000+ business test group) whenever ready, and only gate general availability behind a templated evidence checklist.
insights.speaker: Christine Deakers
insights.so_what: A reusable operating playbook: the speed-vs-quality tradeoff disappears once release decisions are split into two gates, letting AI-accelerated teams ship fast without sacrificing control.
insights.quote: Speed and quality stop being a tradeoff the moment you have two release tiers.
insights.locator: Key insights AI-pilled engineering teams need to know

insights.claim: Ramp's general-availability gate requires a templated checklist (what was built and why, a sub-3-minute Loom demo, early-access KPIs, customer feedback, first-time user journey, sales/support readiness, and a rollout plan) much of which is AI-automated against Ramp's systems, with leaders reviewing within 48 hours or letting the feature ship.
insights.speaker: Christine Deakers
insights.so_what: Shows how AI plus a structured gate lets leadership keep high standards at daily-ship cadence; the 48-hour-or-auto-ship rule prevents review from becoming the bottleneck.
insights.quote: reviewing within 48 hours or letting the feature ship.
insights.locator: 1. How Ramp ships new features daily

themes.theme: Standardize infra not tools (Shopify LLM proxy)

insights.claim: Shopify standardized the infrastructure layer instead of the AI tool: an internal LLM proxy routes all requests from Claude Code, Copilot, Cursor, Codex, or any other tool through a single gateway before the model, giving leadership centralized cost control, per-team/project usage analytics, and model-switching ability without forcing engineers into one workflow.
insights.speaker: Christine Deakers
insights.so_what: Validates the LLM-gateway/proxy category as the durable buy in a market where no single coding tool or model has won; vendors selling routing, cost analytics, and governance sit above the model churn.
insights.quote: Shopify built an LLM proxy that routes all AI requests through one gateway, giving leadership cost control and usage analytics without forcing engineers into a single workflow.
insights.locator: Key insights AI-pilled engineering teams need to know

insights.claim: Shopify deliberately uses many AI tools because no model or workflow has won yet, breaking its own 'one tool for one job' rule only for AI.
insights.speaker: Farhan Thawar
insights.so_what: Signals that enterprise AI-coding spend will stay multi-vendor and unconsolidated near-term; bet on the abstraction layer, not on a single winning model or IDE.
insights.quote: At Shopify, we always have one tool for one job, except for with AI. Since we don't know yet which company, workflow, or model is going to win.
insights.locator: 2. How Shopify enabled AI tool experimentation

insights.claim: Shopify connects AI to internal systems via MCP servers, letting engineers query Salesforce, Slack, GitHub, and internal wikis through AI assistants using the same access controls as their normal auth flow, so infrastructure governs access rather than individual engineers.
insights.speaker: Christine Deakers
insights.so_what: Concrete enterprise MCP-in-production reference; the auth-inheritance pattern is how AI access scales without new governance burden, a template for MCP infra and security vendors.
insights.quote: Through MCP servers, engineers can query Salesforce, Slack, GitHub, and internal wikis through AI assistants, with the same access controls as their normal auth flow.
insights.locator: 2. How Shopify enabled AI tool experimentation

insights.claim: Farhan Thawar drove broad AI adoption by modeling rather than mandating it, framing AI as leverage ('Look how lazy I am') instead of proof of capability, which spread AI to non-engineering teams: sales reps building dashboards, finance building workflow tools, and HR generating 'n-of-1' software.
insights.speaker: Farhan Thawar
insights.so_what: The 'AI as leverage' framing expands the buyer beyond engineering to every department, supporting the thesis that horizontal AI-tooling TAM is larger than dev-only.
insights.quote: I didn't say look at how much work I did and how smart I am. I said, 'Look how lazy I am.'
insights.locator: Farhan didn't mandate AI adoption. He modeled it.

insights.claim: Farhan Thawar argues the 2026 engineering move is 'agentic harnesses' — multiple AI agents running in parallel on different parts of a codebase with engineers orchestrating, reviewing, discarding, and merging — and warns teams that don't learn to harness agents in 2026 will fall behind.
insights.speaker: Farhan Thawar
insights.so_what: A named-operator prediction that agent orchestration becomes table stakes within the year; tailwind for agent-harness, orchestration, and parallel-agent infra startups.
insights.quote: If you don't figure out how to harness the agents in 2026, you'll be behind.
insights.locator: 3. Agentic development changes the leadership calculus

insights.claim: Agentic development shifts engineering leadership from managing people who write code to managing systems that orchestrate AI that writes code, changing the required skills, infrastructure, and failure modes.
insights.speaker: Christine Deakers
insights.so_what: Reframes the org and tooling spend around orchestration and agent-safety infra; the scarce hire is a leader fluent in both people-scaling and the AI transition.
insights.quote: engineering leadership moves from managing people who write code to managing systems that orchestrate AI that writes code.
insights.locator: 3. Agentic development changes the leadership calculus

insights.claim: Two agentic patterns are already emerging: parallel agents working different codebase parts with human review, and extended sequential critique loops where a single model runs deep reasoning cycles of 45 minutes or more, generating, evaluating, and refining its own work.
insights.speaker: Christine Deakers
insights.so_what: The 45-minute self-critique loop is a concrete signal that long-horizon autonomous reasoning is in production, raising token consumption and favoring inference-cost and eval tooling.
insights.quote: a single model runs deep reasoning cycles of 45 minutes or more, generating, evaluating, and refining its own work.
insights.locator: 3. Agentic development changes the leadership calculus

insights.claim: Shopify still requires human review on production code but Farhan Thawar acknowledges that as AI output quality improves, that requirement will change too.
insights.speaker: Farhan Thawar
insights.so_what: Flags a forward trajectory toward reduced human-in-the-loop on production code, the key threshold for fully autonomous coding economics.
insights.quote: The human review requirement on production code remains (for now).
insights.locator: 3. Agentic development changes the leadership calculus

insights.claim: Operating advisor Jessica Popp's framework: seed-to-10-engineers needs a hands-on shipper comfortable with scarcity (architecture sense is a bonus); 10-to-20 is when 'one-way door' architectural decisions (data store, DevOps, testing, quality structure) get locked in and need someone who has made them before; 20-to-50 is the 'loud signal' trap where leadership mismatch surfaces only after cultural or architectural damage.
insights.speaker: Jessica Popp
insights.so_what: A diagnostic for founders and investors on engineering-leadership fit by stage; mis-timed leadership is a predictable, costly failure mode VCs can screen for in portfolio companies.
insights.quote: Anything that impacts your team's day-to-day operations can become a core part of your engineering organization's culture.

insights.claim: At 20+ engineers the decision is not whether to change leadership but to diagnose whether challenges are people-related (bring in a leader who has scaled an org) or product/architecture-related (the founding CTO is often still right), then structurally define how CTO relates to a new VP/SVP of Engineering in advance.
insights.speaker: Jessica Popp
insights.so_what: Gives investors a concrete intervention at the 20-50 inflection: force explicit CTO/SVP role definition rather than defaulting to replacing the founder.
insights.quote: The CTO might decide: 'I'm going to run a two-person architecture team and set the technology vision, and the SVP of Engineering will manage the other 50 or so people and organize them to execute on the vision,'

insights.claim: BVP frames 'comprehension debt' as the hidden tax of AI-speed adoption: engineers who ship fast but can't diagnose why something broke or reason about system behavior without AI; reversion rates won't surface it, but weekly demos will because they reveal whether teams understand what they're building.
insights.speaker: Christine Deakers
insights.so_what: Names a new measurable risk category for AI-native orgs; creates demand for comprehension/observability metrics beyond productivity dashboards and is a leading indicator of future maintenance risk in AI-heavy codebases.
insights.quote: Comprehension debt is the hidden tax on AI-speed adoption. Engineers who ship fast but can't diagnose why something broke are accumulating it silently.
insights.locator: Key insights AI-pilled engineering teams need to know

insights.claim: Farhan Thawar's guardrail against comprehension debt is requiring engineers to understand systems two to three layers below where they're actively working — abdicate the toil, not the thinking — even though Shopify's reversion rates on AI-assisted code stayed roughly equivalent to pre-AI baselines.
insights.speaker: Farhan Thawar
insights.so_what: The equivalent reversion-rate data point counters the 'AI lowers code quality' fear; the real risk is human understanding, reframing where engineering leaders should spend oversight.
insights.quote: You shouldn't abdicate the thinking. You should abdicate the toil.
insights.locator: 5. Avoiding the hidden danger of comprehension debt
